quote: I wasn't all that impressed right now. Let me just let me just circle off on on having you reach back out to me, and and, I'll email you and and we'll set up a good time
stewart_read: This is a recycled-lead prior-experience objection, not a changed-mind withdrawal. Jared is telling Brandon exactly why he has been delaying: the prior evaluation didn't land. Brandon's response at 00:55 implicitly reframes by calling this a 'new solar program' — directionally correct per rebuttals.already_saw_design — but he never asks what was unimpressive about the prior evaluation. Strategic read: Brandon made the right implicit move; he just didn't make it explicit. Reactive read: Brandon moved past the objection without resolving it. The strategic read is more supported — Brandon did label it 'new' — but the move needed one more sentence to land.
alternative_hypothesis: Brandon's 'new solar program' framing was purely reactive filler with no strategic intent behind it.
coaching_implication: Show Brandon the 00:41 moment and ask: 'What did Jared just tell you?' The answer is 'why he's been delaying for months.' Brandon made the right implicit move — he needs to make it explicit. Suggested rephrase: 'That's actually why I'm calling — what we're showing now is different from what you saw last year. What was it about that evaluation that didn't land for you?' The closer also needs to know what the prior evaluation showed — this is the single most important piece of context missing from the handoff.

quote: And what we talked about was showing you our new solar program. Mhmm. You don't need to pay anything upfront. We just wanna help you get a lower energy bill. So you just save money up every month.
stewart_read: Brandon mentions 'lower energy bill' but does not anchor to Jared's actual bill amount or flip the bill into emotional alignment-against-the-utility. This is the bill-capture-and-drop failure pattern the codex identifies as universal across the corpus. On a recycled lead where Jared has already seen a prior evaluation, the bill anchor is the one lever that could differentiate this conversation — 'what are you paying now vs what you could be paying' — but Brandon doesn't pull it.
alternative_hypothesis: Brandon may have had Jared's bill on file from the prior evaluation and was intentionally deferring the bill discussion to the closer.
coaching_implication: Even if the bill is on file, Brandon should reference it to create emotional alignment: 'You're still paying [whatever Jared's bill is] every month — that's what we want to change.' The number creates urgency; generic 'lower energy bill' language does not. Flag this as the bill-anchor-without-flip pattern per coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring.

quote: And with the programs that are still available, they help you switch over to solar at no cost to you.
stewart_read: Brandon is pitching program features — 'no cost to you,' 'save money every month,' 'new program' — rather than creating curiosity about Jared's specific situation. This is scope creep into closer-tier content: the setter's job is to schedule, not to sell the program. The feature pitch also risks creating objections (Jared could ask 'what does no cost mean exactly?') that Brandon is not equipped to answer. The codex's curiosity_over_features principle is the corrective: make Jared curious to see his specific math, not sold on a generic program.
alternative_hypothesis: On a recycled lead with a prior-evaluation disappointment, some re-engagement pitch may be necessary to re-establish interest before scheduling.
coaching_implication: Brandon's instinct to re-engage Jared is correct; the execution is scope-creeping. Coach him toward curiosity framing: 'The reason I'm calling is that what we're showing people now is different from what you saw — I want to get you an updated look so you can see if the numbers make sense for your home.' This creates curiosity without selling features or making promises the closer has to honor.

quote: I can have a special just meet with you if you're available this Saturday.
stewart_read: Brandon pushes Saturday before Jared has agreed to any time — and Jared had already signaled 'next week' at 00:32. This is a listening gap: Brandon is running a scheduling script rather than following Jared's stated availability. The Saturday push gets a flat 'No, man' rejection and costs Brandon a beat of rapport. The correct move at this point was to ask 'What does next week look like for you?' and let Jared name the window.
alternative_hypothesis: Brandon may have been testing whether Jared would take an earlier slot if offered, as a way to accelerate the sit.
coaching_implication: Brandon had the information he needed at 00:32 ('next week') and ignored it. Show him the sequence: Jared says 'next week' at 00:32, Brandon pushes Saturday at 01:28, Jared says no. The lesson is not 'don't offer Saturday' — it's 'listen to what the customer already told you.' When Jared finally names next Wednesday at 01:43, Brandon locks it immediately — that's the right move. Coach him to get there without the rejected Saturday detour.

quote: Yeah. I can set up an appointment for next Wednesday to meet with you.
stewart_read: This is the pivot that unlocks the booking. After the Saturday rejection, Brandon follows Jared's lead to next Wednesday and immediately moves to lock a time. The pivot is clean — no argument, no second push on Saturday, no feature re-pitch. Brandon simply meets Jared where he is. This is the move that converts a soft-exit attempt into a booked appointment and should be reinforced explicitly.
coaching_implication: Reinforce this moment in the 1-on-1: 'This is where you won the call. You stopped pushing your agenda and followed his. That's the move.' Pair it with the Saturday push at 01:28 to show the contrast — the lesson is that the second approach (follow the customer's window) worked; the first (push your preferred slot) didn't.

quote: I can have someone just give you a call if you prefer that, or we can meet with you in person.
stewart_read: Brandon correctly offers the phone option per button_up.phone_appointment_specified, and Jared immediately confirms 'Phone call is fine.' This is clean protocol execution — Brandon does not assume in-person, gives Jared the choice, and Jared self-selects the lower-friction channel. Worth reinforcing as a positive execution of the button-up protocol.
coaching_implication: Flag this as a positive in the 1-on-1. Brandon's instinct to offer the choice rather than default to in-person is correct and reduces friction. Pair with the missing elements of button_up (no-driving anchor, computer anchor, 'is that fair' handshake) to show what the full protocol looks like around this one piece he got right.

quote: I'll I'll I'll set it up, where I send, like, a little reminder that I have the electric bill. So that way I can shoot it over and give it things like that.
stewart_read: Jared volunteers to send the bill unprompted — he is not just agreeing to the appointment, he is actively planning his own follow-through. This is a meaningful engagement signal on a customer who spent the first half of the call trying to defer. Brandon's response ('Yeah. I sent you a text') acknowledges it but does not anchor on it. The correct move is to reinforce Jared's ownership: 'Perfect — that's exactly what we need. Once I have that, our specialist can build your specific numbers before the call.'
coaching_implication: Show Brandon this moment as a signal he walked past. Jared just told him 'I'm invested enough to prepare.' Brandon's job here is to reinforce that investment and tie it to the appointment's value: the bill makes the evaluation specific to Jared's home. A suggested rephrase: 'Perfect — that bill is what makes the difference between a generic estimate and your actual numbers. Send it over whenever you have it and I'll make sure our specialist has it ready.' This also reduces no-show risk by giving Jared a pre-appointment task that creates commitment.

quote: 3PM. Next Wednesday, I'll do 2PM. Right now. Yep.
stewart_read: The time confirmation is muddled — Jared says '3PM' at 01:57, Brandon confirms '3PM' at 02:49, then immediately says '2PM' in the same breath. Most likely a verbal stumble rather than a genuine competing time proposal, but the confirmed time is ambiguous going into the closer's calendar. Additionally, the full button_up protocol is incomplete: no no-driving anchor, no computer anchor, no 'is that fair' handshake. The booking is real but the structural reinforcements that predict show rate are missing.
alternative_hypothesis: Brandon may have been confirming a different time slot he had available, making 2PM the actual booked time.
coaching_implication: Two separate coaching beats here. First, the time ambiguity: coach Brandon to always read back the confirmed time as the last thing he says before hanging up — 'So I've got you down for next Wednesday the fifteenth at 3PM — does that work?' Second, walk through the full button_up checklist with Brandon and identify which elements he completed (phone channel, datetime) vs which he skipped (no-driving anchor, computer anchor, 'is that fair'). Each skipped element is a no-show risk multiplier on an already-fragile sit.
